title: Νέα Ἑλληνικά — the living end of the continuum
blurb: The same language, still spoken. These show how Greek sounds today — and where the modern tongue kept, dropped, or reshaped what the ancient texts do.

phrases.en: Good morning! How are you? (lit. 'what are you doing?')
phrases.note: καλή + ἡμέρα 'good day' — the ancient ἡμέρα (→ 'ephemeral') survives intact. The question particle-free 'τι κάνεις;' shows the modern semicolon (;) used as a QUESTION mark.
εὐχαριστῶ 'I give thanks' — the very verb behind Eucharist (εὐ 'well' + χάρις 'grace'). χάρις also appears in John 1:14, 16. Ancient meaning preserved, everyday register.

Voice-gender safeguard in action: the predicate adjective agrees with the SPEAKER'S gender, so the male and female voices each say their own form. A male voice may never utter κουρασμένη, nor a female κουρασμένος.
Modern εἶμαι 'I am' descends straight from ancient εἰμί (John 1:1 ἦν is its imperfect). Again the self-form is gendered: ἕτοιμος / ἑτοίμη.

θάλασσα is the same word Xenophon's soldiers cried on sight of the sea (θάλαττα in Attic — note the Attic -ττ- vs. common -σσ-). The article ἡ → modern η, but note the DATIVE case has vanished in Modern Greek: where Koine says ἐν τῇ (dative), modern Greek uses σε + accusative.
